title: Fraunhofer Patterns in Atomic Josephson Junctions
authors: Kevin T. Geier, Giampiero Marchegiani, Vijay Pal Singh, Juan Polo, Luigi Amico
abstract: Driven atomic Josephson junctions allow one to monitor phase-coherent dynamics with unprecedented control and flexibility of the system's physical conditions. While cold-atom manifestations of the Josephson effect have been extensively studied in a wide variety of settings, atomic Josephson junctions in synthetic electromagnetic fields remain largely unexplored. Here, we show that synthetic magnetic fields can induce Fraunhofer-like modulations of the critical current in atomic Josephson junctions. Although this effect presents analogies to the Fraunhofer patterns found in superconducting devices, distinctive features emerge due to the neutral nature of the superfluid. We investigate the underlying spatial interference mechanisms and elucidate the role of Josephson vortices in the formation of spatially modulated current distributions based on numerical simulations. Our results open up new avenues for matter-wave circuits to deepen our understanding of spatial coherence in Josephson junctions, which are fundamental to the development of novel quantum technologies.
summary: This paper explores how synthetic magnetic fields can induce Fraunhofer-like interference patterns in the critical current of atomic Josephson junctions. Using Gross-Pitaevskii simulations, the authors show that while the pattern is analogous to superconducting junctions, the neutral nature of the BEC leads to distinct features like shifted interference minima. The study also elucidates how the entry of Josephson vortices modulates the current density. These findings are significant for developing matter-wave circuits and atomtronic quantum technologies.
main_problem: Investigating the emergence of Fraunhofer-like interference patterns in the critical current of atomic Josephson junctions under synthetic magnetic fields and understanding the role of Josephson vortices.
main_result: The study demonstrates that synthetic magnetic fields induce Fraunhofer-like modulations of the critical current in neutral superfluids, with deviations from ideal superconducting behavior due to the lack of Meissner screening and the presence of large-scale superflows.
method: Numerical simulations based on the time-dependent Gross-Pitaevskii equation (GPE) using sine pseudospectral discretization and Magnus integration.
model_or_system: A quasi-2D Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC) in a rectangular box trap, featuring an atomic Josephson junction created by a repulsive Gaussian potential barrier and subjected to a synthetic vector potential.
key_observables: Critical current (Ic), chemical potential difference (delta-mu), current density distribution (J(y)), synthetic magnetic flux (Phi), and vortex count.
important_parameters: Synthetic magnetic flux (Phi/Phi0), barrier velocity (v), healing length (xi), chemical potential (mu), and the DC/AC regimes.
main_assumptions: The system is in the deep tunneling regime where the current can be approximated by the barrier velocity; the synthetic gauge field does not experience reciprocal effects (no screening) from the neutral matter.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the experimental setup; Figure 2 illustrates the temporal evolution of density and vortex entry during flux sweeps; Figure 3 shows signatures of Josephson vortices; Figure 4 displays the current-phase relation; Figure S8 compares wave numbers extracted from current density and phase difference.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the setup of atomic Josephson junctions in synthetic fields, presents numerical results for critical current modulation (Fraunhofer patterns), analyzes the microscopic role of Josephson vortices, compares extraction methods for critical current, and discusses the impact of superflows on phase gradients.
why_interesting: It provides a bridge between superconductivity and atomtronics, showing how neutral superfluids can mimic complex electromagnetic phenomena like Fraunhofer patterns while introducing unique physics due to the absence of the Meissner effect.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Fraunhofer pattern in an atomic Josephson junction. (a) The junction is created by a repulsive barrier potential, forming a density depletion (light color) in a quasi-2D Bose–Einstein condensate with particle density n0 and dimensions Lx × Ly (dark color). Moving the barrier at a constant velocity v induces a net particle current I ≈−vn0Ly through the junction. A synthetic magnetic field B = B(x −vt)ˆz localized around the junction (red curve) induces a gradient of the phase difference φ(y) across the junction (dashed white line) and therefore a spatial modulation of the current density J(y) at the junction. (b) Chemical potential difference ∆µ versus barrier velocity v for...

Figure 2. Microscopic dynamics underlying the formation of Fraunhofer patterns in atomic Josephson junctions. (a) The magnetic flux Φ is slowly increased in a sequence of consec- utive sweeps. (b) Temporal evolution of the density n(x0, y) at the junction. Each sweep of the magnetic field to a new lobe of the Fraunhofer pattern is accompanied by the entry of an additional vortex into the junction from below, such that the nth lobe features n vortices. (c) Snaptshots of the den- sity n(x0, y) at values of Φ marked by vertical dashed lines in panel (b).

Figure 3. Signatures of Josephson vortices. The solid lines in each row show the phase difference φ(y) as well as its first and second derivatives for two values of the magnetic flux Φ corresponding to the respective configurations in Fig. 2. The location of the phase kinks, visible as peaks in ∂yφ, coincides with the position of vortex cores. The dashed lines show the right-hand side of the sine-Gordon (SG) equation, λ−2 J sin φ, with λJ obtained from fits to ∂2 yφ and integration constants determined by fits to ∂yφ and φ. In the single-vortex case (left column), the kink locally fits the phase profile of a Josephson vortex on a linear background.

Figure 4. Current–phase relation as an alternative method for probing the critical current Ic. The solid lines represent fits of the Josephson relation I(φ0) = Ic sin φ0 to the simulation data (markers) with Ic as a fit parameter. The barrier veloc- ity v is encoded in the lightness of the colors. For an even (odd) number of vortices inside the junction, the current– phase relation is centered around φ0 = 0 (φ0 = π).

Figure 5. Comparison of Fraunhofer-like patterns extracted from the current–phase versus voltage–current relation. The dots represent the critical current Ic extracted by fitting the Josephson relation I(φ0) = Ic sin φ0 to the integral current– phase relation in Fig. 4, while the triangles correspond to Ic ≈|vc|n0Ly, where the critical velocity vc has been ex- tracted from the voltage–current relation as in Fig. 1(b). In the main lobe, the extrapolated current–phase relation sys- tematically overestimates the critical current realizable in practice with our protocol due to dynamical instabilities.
